The process also has high energy-efficiency since the reactions are carried out at atmospheric pressure, and it employs biodegradable chemicals that do not accumulate in the environment.
companies.potential_impact: Affordable nanocellulose provides a tangible substitute for petroleum-based materials in high-volume applications such as packaging, automotive manufacturing, construction, and more. Replacing petroleum-based materials with nanocellulose in such vast markets will reduce the CO2 emissions associated with both their production and disposal. By using agricultural wastes as feedstock, VERDE Nanomaterials will also be converting the atmospheric CO2 captured by those plants into long-lasting, valuable biomaterials, supporting a circular bioeconomy.

fellow_profiles.name: Danielle Pascoli
fellow_profiles.bio: Danielle Pascoli completed her Ph.D. at the University of Washington (UW) where she built expertise in biomass conversion to biofuels and biomaterials with a focus on process development and sustainability. She earned a bachelor’s degree in biochemical engineering from the University of São Paulo in Brazil and a master’s degree from UW. VERDE Nanomaterials is a result of her doctoral research in which she developed a cost-effective process to manufacture nanocellulose using agricultural wastes.

companies.name: Vycarb
companies.tagline: Empowering the world with high-quality, high-impact carbon removal.
companies.one_liner: Vycarb makes autonomous systems that measure, remove, and store CO₂ in water.
companies.elevator_pitch: There are lots of emerging technologies out there to try to remove CO₂ from the atmosphere, but no one really knows how effective they are. Vycarb's approach fully quantifies how much CO₂ is removed and stored in water to ensure that climate solutions are real, safe, and effective.
companies.critical_need: Billions of tons of carbon dioxide can be removed from the environment by applying alkaline materials to water and soils. Current application methods, however, cannot be done at scale, and lack reliable verification tools. A new technology to measure and verify the efficacy of alkalinity at scale, which offset purchasers and regulatory agencies require, and deploy the practice to advance ecosystem health, is critically needed.
companies.technology_vision: Vycarb has a new way to verify the efficacy of alkalinity for carbon dioxide removal, and a new way to apply alkalinity that restores ecosystem health. Vycarb’s measurement device is designed to monitor inorganic carbon, the stable carbon generated from alkalinity carbon dioxide removal, at low-cost and low-maintenance to scale across geographies. Vycarb’s application approach targets ecosystems where high carbon dioxide harms local ecology, and can drive fast, high-volume carbon dioxide removal, providing both near-term and long-term solutions to ecologies and global climate change.
companies.potential_impact: By coupling novel measurement technology to ecologically restorative alkalinity application, Vycarb is creating climate solutions that achieve both climate change adaptation and mitigation and can scale to billion-ton carbon dioxide removal. Carbon dioxide removal projects requiring field-based measurements can leverage Vycarb’s low-cost, reliable measurement device to demonstrate effective climate change mitigation, creating high-value carbon offsets. Vycarb’s own deployment will improve ecosystem health and quality to advance climate resilience and adaptation.

fellow_profiles.name: Garrett Boudinot
fellow_profiles.bio: Garrett Boudinot is the founder and CEO of Vycarb, an early-stage startup on a mission to empower the world with high-quality, high-impact carbon dioxide removal. Climate change has been the sole focus of Boudinot’s career, from carbon chemistry and ecosystem impacts to social adaptation and wildlife conservation. Boudinot earned his Ph.D. at the University of Colorado, holds dual bachelor's degrees in humanities and environmental geosciences from the College of Charleston, and served as a research associate at Cornell University. Boudinot also serves on the New York State Climate Impacts Assessment and the ClimateMusic Project.

companies.name: Wild Microbes
companies.one_liner: Wild Microbes is domesticating and engineering wild bacteria, and building a strain catalog that will open new possibilities for biomanufacturing.
companies.elevator_pitch: Wild Microbes is unlocking microbial biodiversity. WM's core technology allows the company to quickly domesticate promising microbes and engineer them for specific purposes. WM will both partner with other bioeconomy players and develop a proprietary strain catalog filled with exceptional hosts that allow robust, cost-competitive scaling of diverse industrial bioprocesses.
companies.critical_need: Wild Microbes is tackling entrenched challenges in industrial bioproduction. Frequent issues arise when scaling bioprocesses, including batch loss, process robustness, and elevated cost structures. Solutions to these challenges are currently limited by the narrow host range available to most product companies. Hundreds of microbes can be found in just a teaspoon of soil, but bioproduction relies on only a handful of well-studied laboratory strains. Leaving the vast majority of wild microbes on the sidelines artificially limits industry’s ability to solve pressing challenges. The key to unlocking these sidelined wild microbes is effective genetic tooling.
companies.technology_vision: Using a suite of genetic tools, Wild is developing a catalog of diverse, industrial-ready microbial strains. This catalog will feature “natural athletes” of all kinds, including extremophiles, microbes with diverse metabolic profiles, and microbes that consume renewable feedstocks. While building its microbial catalog, Wild will partner with companies throughout the bioeconomy to accelerate their strain engineering efforts. For instance, Wild will help agricultural biotech companies to rapidly engineer microbes they identify in the plant rhizosphere for carbon or nitrogen fixation.
companies.potential_impact: In the effort to decarbonize every part of the economy, two sectors have been particularly difficult to change: heavy industry and agriculture. Synthetic biology can impact these sectors, and Wild Microbes will act as an accelerant to many of the companies leveraging solutions that depend on microbes. The largest impact area will be to unlock bioproduction of commodity chemicals, fuels, and foods cost competitively, and at scale. This will allow the economy to shift away from oil and natural gas as its primary physical input and toward renewable feedstocks such as carbon dioxide, methane, and agricultural waste.

nsf_awards.title: SBIR Phase I:  Developing new bacterial hosts for productive secretion of difficult-to-express proteins by precision fermentation

fellow_profiles.name: Tim Wannier
fellow_profiles.bio: Timothy Wannier is co-founder and CEO of Wild Microbes. He earned a Ph.D. in protein biophysics from the California Institute of Technology and was a postdoctoral researcher at Harvard, where he developed advanced microbial engineering tools and piloted them in more than a dozen bacterial species. With Wild Microbes, Wannier is translating his focus on microbial biodiversity to applications ranging from biological production of sustainable products to the engineering of soil microbes.

companies.name: Ọsọ Semiconductor
companies.one_liner: Ọsọ Semiconductor is an innovative fabless semiconductor company selling ultralow-power, performance microchips to supercharge the antennas of the future.
companies.elevator_pitch: Ọsọ Semiconductor is an innovative fabless semiconductor company selling ultralow-power, low-cost, performance microchips to supercharge the antennas of the future. Its patentable ultra-low loss beamformer, featuring a Combiner-First™ architecture, provides massive power and cost savings for antenna manufacturers.
companies.critical_need: The high cost and power consumption of electronic components in Electrically Steerable Antennas (ESAs) are greatly limiting growth for several high-impact wireless applications.<BREAK>In the Satellite Communication (SatCom) antenna market, analysts and CEOs have publicly decried the high cost of ESAs as the biggest challenge facing the industry. Estimates indicate that wireless microchips represent the majority of total cost and of power consumption. This is a huge problem for ESA manufacturers and integrated satellite operators. And, it’s not just SatCom; 5G and automotive radar markets also use ESAs and could benefit from more cost- and power- effective solutions.
companies.technology_vision: Ọsọ Semiconductor’s ultra-low power wireless chips, featuring its Combiner-First™ architecture, allows customers to build high-performance ESAs that consume 75 percent less power and lower electronic component costs by 40 percent. This innovative startup is developing a patentable beamformer technology that is many times less lossy than state-of-the-art technology. It plans to combine this beamformer with advanced amplifiers to produce a front-end wireless chipset that is unlike anything in the market.
companies.potential_impact: Low-cost, low-power antennas using Ọsọ microchips could thrust millions of Americans living in remote and underdeveloped areas into the digital economy. Democratizing access to Web3.0-speed connections could change the lives of hundreds of millions more people in the developing world, leveling the playing field. We can never know where the next Steve Jobs or Katherine Johnson will be born, but we do know that they will need to be connected to the networks of the future to reach their full potential. Ọsọ’s technology is critical to making that happen!

fellow_profiles.name: Matthew Anderson
fellow_profiles.bio: Matthew G. Anderson is founder and CEO of Ọsọ Semiconductor, an innovative fabless semiconductor company selling ultralow-power, low-cost, performance microchips to supercharge the antennas of the future. Previously, he was an electrical engineering and computer science Ph.D. student at the University of California, Berkeley, specializing in low-power wireless microchip design. Anderson has more than six years of industry experience at Apple and other tech companies as well as a bachelor’s degree in electrical engineering and physics.

companies.name: Anthro
companies.one_liner: Anthro Energy is changing the shape of energy storage to enable the electrification of everything.
companies.elevator_pitch: Anthro Energy is changing the shape of energy storage. Its highly robust polymer electrolyte easily integrates into existing battery manufacturing facilities and enables flexibility and safety while maintaining excellent performance. Ultimately, Anthro technology will enable structural batteries that can be integrated into the design of any electrical system.

fellow_profiles.name: David Mackanic
fellow_profiles.bio: David Mackanic is founder and CEO of Anthro Energy, a company inventing next-generation polymers to create batteries that are flexible, safe, and high performance. He earned his Ph.D. in chemical engineering from Stanford University with a focus on polymer science and electrochemistry. He was listed on Forbes 30 Under 30 in Energy for 2021, and was awarded the MRS Gold Award and the ACS Bright Science Award.

companies.name: Aura Intelligent Systems
companies.tagline: Digital Imaging Radar for Safe, Autonomous Mobility
companies.one_liner: Aura Intelligent Systems is developing a high-resolution imaging radar for automotive safety and automation with patented digital technology.
companies.elevator_pitch: Autonomous vehicles and robots need to be able to perceive their surroundings in harsh environments. But today's radars do not provide sufficiently high-resolution sensing and are susceptible to harmful interference in urban environments. Aura is developing a high-resolution digital imaging radar technology to make cars and autonomous navigation safer.

nsf_awards.title: SBIR Phase I:  Digital Imaging Radar for Autonomy Infrastructure

fellow_profiles.name: Jungah Lee
fellow_profiles.bio: Jungah Lee is a co-founder and CEO of Aura Intelligent Systems, a company disrupting wireless systems with patented 4D sensing technology. She previously led advanced technology for 4G/5G networks at Samsung Networks and Altiostar and worked at Lucent Technologies-Bell Labs. She holds a B.S. and M.S. degrees in electronics engineering from Seoul National University, and a Ph.D. in electrical and computer engineering from the University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign.

companies.name: Electrified Thermal Solutions
companies.one_liner: Electrified Thermal Solutions is decarbonizing industries with on-demand renewable heat.
companies.elevator_pitch: Electrified Thermal Solutions is developing the Joule Hive: a new energy storage technology that turns unreliable solar and wind energy into on-demand heat. The Joule Hive stores and delivers up to 1800C air, tapping into a $3 trillion heat market and enabling the path to decarbonization by 2050.
companies.critical_need: Burning fossil fuels to generate heat and electricity on-demand produces an enormous amount of carbon emissions. Wind and solar are cleaner but intermittent and can only deliver electricity, not the 1700-2000°C needed for intense industrial processes and to run efficient compressed-gas turbines. A technology that converts intermittent electricity into constant high heat could transform the energy industry. Known options to store renewable electricity thermally, such as hydrogen, hot rocks, carbon blocks, or molten salts, are capital-intensive, not very efficient, or both. There is an urgent need for highly efficient, cost-effective options to produce carbon-free heat for steel and cement production and on-demand electricity for the grid.
companies.technology_vision: Renewables are getting cost-competitive with fossil fuels, but storing them is still costly. ETS’s technology, the Joule Hive, can store gigawatt-hours of excess renewable energy as 2000°C heat more efficiently and cheaply than competitive thermal storage technologies. Based on electrically conductive ceramics developed at MIT, the Joule Hive has a unique ability to store and deliver flame-hot energy at high efficiency. This zero-carbon heat is an ideal replacement for the dirty fuels burned in industrial furnaces and kilns and in power plants that run turbines to deliver on-demand electricity. The product creates value for renewable power generators, existing power plant owners, and turbine manufacturers, even as it helps decarbonize industry and the grid.
companies.potential_impact: Producing steel and cement contributes about 15 percent of global carbon emissions, while electricity production contributes far more. ETS’s technology to store renewable electricity as intense heat could decarbonize all these sectors. And it could do so cost-effectively thanks to an efficient, easily accessible heat stored in novel conductive ceramics. By allowing traditional power plants and industrial plants to store and use renewable electricity, the technology could help forge a path to a 100 percent zero-carbon energy transition and mitigate myriad health effects from poor air quality.

fellow_profiles.name: Dan Stack
fellow_profiles.bio: A recent Ph.D. recipient and aspiring entrepreneur in zero-carbon energy, Daniel Stack has spent a decade studying energy conversion and energy systems and seeks to commercialize the Joule Hive technology, originally developed during his time at MIT. His experience includes ceramic engineering and fabrication, heat and mass transfer simulations, design optimization, and techno-economic analyses.

fellow_profiles.name: Joey Kabel
fellow_profiles.bio: Joey Kabel earned a Ph.D. in nuclear engineering, focused on experimental test development for high temperature ceramics from UC Berkeley. As a material scientist, he is passionate about applying the fundamentals of "cook and look" and "heat and beat" to solve our world's most pressing challenges. As a climate enthusiast, he hopes to leverage his experiences to shape the future of energy consumption.

companies.name: Enertia Microsystems
companies.one_liner: Enertia Microsystems Inc. provides low-cost, lightweight, and high-precision navigation solutions for future mobility, wearable, and space applications.
companies.elevator_pitch: Enertia Microsystems Inc. is commercializing affordable, lightweight, and ultra-high-precision navigation solutions for position and orientation measurement indoors, in GPS-denied environments, and in harsh weather conditions where vision-based sensors lose accuracy. The Birdbath Resonator Gyroscope has 10,000x higher accuracy than micro gyroscopes currently on the market.

nsf_awards.title: SBIR Phase I:  Commercialization of low-cost micro birdbath resonant gyroscope

fellow_profiles.name: Jae Cho
fellow_profiles.bio: Jae Cho is the CEO of Enertia Microsystems, which is commercializing low-cost, ultra-high-accuracy micro-electro-mechanical systems (MEMS) inertial sensors. During his Ph.D. and postdoctoral training in electrical engineering at the University of Michigan, Cho co-invented a novel ultra-high precision MEMS gyroscope technology called the Birdbath Resonator Gyroscope (RBG). He co-founded Enertia Microsystems with his academic advisor, Khalil Najafi, to commercialize BRG technology.

companies.name: FLO Materials
companies.tagline: Fully Circular Polymers, Enabling Circular Design
companies.one_liner: FLO Materials is commercializing a new class of infinitely recyclable polymers to enable the transition to a more circular economy.
companies.elevator_pitch: FLO Materials is commercializing a new polymer platform (PDKs) that enables recyclability for products that are currently challenging to recycle. PDKs are crosslinked polymers that can be mechanically reprocessed like thermoplastics due to their dynamic nature and chemically recycled to regain their original monomers. Spun out from Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory, FLO Materials will bring to market an infinitely recyclable network of polymers to enable sustainable manufacturing, increase materials efficiency, and facilitate the transition to a more circular plastics economy.
companies.critical_need: Manufacturing consumes two percent of U.S. energy to produce 300 million metric tons of virgin plastics annually. While packaging represents their most common application, plastics are ubiquitous in durable consumer goods, such as footwear, other accessories, textiles, household products, sporting goods, and consumer electronics. Yet, unlike with packaging, consumers generally landfill these products when done with them since virtually no end-of-life markets exist for durable plastics. To support consumer demand for durable goods while also building circular, efficient processes and supply chains with zero waste, manufacturers and brands need a new class of high-performance plastic and products designed for disassembly and remanufacturing.
companies.technology_vision: FLO Materials’ core technology is a new, infinitely recyclable material called polydiketoenamine (PDK), which will enable closed-loop lifecycles for hard-to-recycle plastics, including thermosets and mixed material composites, used in durable goods. Chemical recycling of PDKs through depolymerization allows manufacturers to remove all additives, such as colors or plasticizers, and then remanufacture the recovered monomers into next-generation materials, without losing mechanical performance or aesthetic quality. Plus, recycled PDKs are lower-cost than virgin PDKs, which will incentivize manufacturers to capture and recycle hard-to-recycle waste from subtractive manufacturing processes, such as products cut from sheets of composite plastics and other complex manufacturing waste. FLO aims to help brands, designers, and the manufacturing industry transition to a more circular economy through a broad portfolio of infinitely recyclable materials and access to manufacturing partners. FLO is building a business model based on recycling as a service: a systems approach integrating material development, product design, reverse logistics, and lifecycle management of consumer goods.
companies.potential_impact: By providing a portfolio of recyclable materials and offering recycling as a service, FLO will empower brands and manufacturers to design products and systems that will accelerate the transition to a zero-waste economy. For just one example, premium eyewear frames are produced from hard-to-recycle composite polymers and manufactured using CNC machining that cuts the material from sheets, generating up to 90 percent waste. FLO Materials estimates that this type of inefficiency results in 25M kg of manufacturing waste globally, costing the industry more than $750M each year. By transitioning to PDK material, manufacturing waste could be collected and recycled back into supply, a huge boost to material efficiency and significant reduction to manufacturers’ carbon footprint.

nsf_awards.title: SBIR Phase II: Dynamic Covalent Polymers for Transition to Closed-loop Plastics Economy
STTR Phase I: Dynamic Covalent Polymers for Transition to Circular Plastics Economy

fellow_profiles.name: Kezi Cheng
fellow_profiles.bio: Kezi Cheng is CEO of FLO Materials. She was recently named to the Forbes 30 Under 30 list in the manufacturing and industry category. Previously, she earned a Ph.D. from the Harvard John A. Paulson School of Engineering and Applied Sciences. She was an NSF Graduate Research Fellow, a Harvard Graduate Energy and Environment Consortium Fellow, and was on the organizing team for the inaugural Harvard Circular Economy Symposium. Cheng holds a bachelor's degree in materials science and engineering with a minor in management from MIT.

companies.name: Inlyte Energy
companies.tagline: Affordable Grid Batteries from Iron and Salt
companies.one_liner: Inlyte Energy stores solar energy with batteries made from abundant materials that are safe, long-lasting, and efficient.
companies.elevator_pitch: Harnessing abundant, economic, and clean energy from renewables requires low-cost and reliable energy storage technologies. The commercialized Zebra battery has demonstrated safety, efficiency, and long lifetime. Inlyte Energy is delivering the next evolution in this technology by using the abundant materials salt and iron to create ultra-low cost grid storage.

fellow_profiles.name: Antonio Baclig
fellow_profiles.bio: Antonio Baclig studied batteries as a Ph.D. and then postdoc in Stanford’s materials science and engineering department, where he spearheaded the research of novel high-energy density electrolytes for flow batteries. Prior to that, he was one of the first employees at a venture-backed clean energy start-up (C12 Energy), working on a wide range of technical and business tasks. Baclig received his bachelor's degree from Harvard in chemistry and physics.

companies.name: Intropic Materials
companies.tagline: Truly Compostable Plastic for a Circular Bioeconomy
companies.one_liner: Intropic Materials is commercializing a groundbreaking enzyme stabilization technology, first being used to make plastics degrade from the inside out.
companies.elevator_pitch: Intropic Materials is unlocking a new bio-active products market with its enzyme stabilizing technology. By embedding degrading enzymes directly inside plastics, Intropic is imparting a favorable end-of-life at its beginning. Once activated, the enzymes trigger the plastic to rapidly and completely self-degrade into biodegradable or chemically recyclable small molecules.
companies.critical_need: Of the roughly 8.3 billion metric tons of plastic produced globally since the 1950s, 6.3 billion metric tons have become waste, with much of it permeating our environment and bodies in the form of microplastics. Bioplastics are a potential solution to this pervasive pollution, with a projected $12.4 billion market in the next five years. However, even certified compostable products require industrial composting facilities that often reject them due to long degradation times. Thus they are usually sent to landfills, or they become contaminants in conventional recycling streams or in nature. Bioplastics are currently failing to deliver their promised impact. Brands, consumers, and waste management need truly compostable, circular materials that work with nature, not against it
companies.technology_vision: Enzymes are nature’s chemists, performing chemical transformations with unparalleled efficiency and specificity. However, their fragility limits their industrial utility. Intropic Materials is using its random heteropolymer (RHP) platform to stabilize enzymes in a range of chemical environments and plastic processing conditions. RHPs contain multiple monomers, each with unique chemical functionality designed to generate self-assembled nanoscale protective coatings, protecting enzymes without compromising their function. Intropic Materials uses this to put degrading enzymes directly inside plastics. Once the material is placed in compost (or triggered by a specific set of environmental conditions), the enzymes completely degrade the plastics into monomers that can be composted or chemically recycled in days rather than months. This approach is highly tunable and solves a long-standing challenge in enzyme-embedded plastics.
companies.potential_impact: Enzymes are central to life, and harnessing their incredible performance and diversity of functions promises to revolutionize sustainable materials, chemical manufacturing, food technology, pharmaceuticals, consumer products, sensors, and beyond. However, for all their utility and our increasing ability to engineer and optimize them, enzyme stability outside of cells remains a key challenge to developing applications that are technically and economically viable outside of the lab. By providing a general, orthogonal, and scalable approach to stabilization, Intropic Materials is working to unlock the potential for a sustainable circular bio-based economy, starting with truly compostable plastics.

fellow_profiles.name: Aaron Hall
fellow_profiles.bio: Aaron Hall is working to solve the plastic waste problem from the inside out. His diverse professional experiences in consumer packaged goods R&D, consulting, financial management, and strategy guide his work as founder of Intropic Materials. Hall earned his Ph.D. in materials science and engineering from UC Berkeley, where he led design and development of the novel enzyme stabilizing polymers at the core of Intropic, and a B.S. in chemistry from UC Santa Cruz.

companies.name: Kano Therapeutics
companies.one_liner: Kano Therapeutics is unlocking the full potential of genetic engineering through purpose-built synthetic single-stranded DNA.
companies.elevator_pitch: Kano Therapeutics harnesses the power of DNA nanotechnology to design and engineer functional gene vectors to expand the reach of genome-editing-based therapies. Instead of innovating around the gene-editing and delivery tools (the messenger), we start by innovating around the DNA vector that carries the genetic information (the message).

federal_agencies.name: Department of Health and Human Services

fellow_profiles.name: Floris Engelhardt
fellow_profiles.bio: Floris Engelhardt is CEO of Kano Therapeutics, which is expanding the reach of genome editing-based therapies. Previously, she was a postdoctoral researcher in the Bathe BioNanoLab at MIT. Engelhardt earned a Ph.D. in physics and a master's degree in molecular biotechnology from the Technical University of Munich. She holds a bachelor's degree in biochemistry and molecular biology from the Friedrich-Schiller University.

companies.name: LintrinsIC
companies.tagline: CMOS RF Switches Facilitating Next-generation Wireless
companies.one_liner: LintrinsIC Semiconductors specializes in high linearity RF and MMW integrated circuits in SOI CMOS for defense and commercial applications.
companies.elevator_pitch: As networks move to increasingly wireless architectures, high-bandwidth and agile radio architectures will replace today's single-purpose radios. Reconfigurable radios have struggled to meet commercial performance demands without drastically increasing in size, power, and cost. LintrinsIC is solving these imitations using novel silicon-on-insulator architectures that outperform costly III-V semiconductors.

fellow_profiles.name: Cameron Hill
fellow_profiles.bio: Cameron Hill's research interests include radio frequency and millimeter-wave integrated circuits, specifically in high linearity CMOS design methodologies, and their applications to reconfigurable radios. Prior to founding LintrinsIC he was a postdoctoral researcher at the University of California at Santa Barbara. His earned his Ph.D. in electrical engineering from UC Santa Barbara in 2020 and his M.E. and B.E. in electrical engineering from Stevens Institute of Technology in Hoboken, New Jersey.

fellow_profiles.name: Pete Christensen
fellow_profiles.bio: Peter Christensen is the co-founder of FLO Materials. While he was a postdoctoral researcher at Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory, Christensen invented the infinitely recyclable polymers known as polydiketoenamines (PDKs) that FLO is commercializing. He holds a Ph.D. in chemistry from the University of British Columbia. Christensen lives in San Diego with his wife and daughter and is currently exploring new projects.

companies.name: Metavoxel Technologies
companies.tagline: Unlocking Metamaterials at the Macro Scale
companies.one_liner: Metavoxel provides scalable metamaterial solutions for efficient and sustainable structures and systems that can do more with less.
companies.elevator_pitch: We need to do more with less, to reduce waste, and to future-proof the $1T+ built environment. Metavoxel can reduce material use by half and offer cost and performance benefits in markets ranging from consumer products to buildings.

fellow_profiles.name: Ben Jenett
fellow_profiles.bio: Ben Jenett completed his Ph.D. at MIT’s Center for Bits and Atoms, where the NASA Space Technology Research Fellowship supported his research. There, he developed discrete mechanical metamaterials, as well as robotic methods for their construction, and demonstrated their real-world applications in transportation, aviation, and aerospace. He has industry background in large scale digital fabrication and advanced structural engineering, and holds a B.A. from UC Berkeley and an M.S. from MIT.

companies.name: Osmoses
companies.tagline: Eliminating energy waste via molecular separation
companies.one_liner: Osmoses is a clean-tech company determined to eliminate energy waste and reduce carbon emissions from industrial separation processes.
companies.elevator_pitch: Osmoses develops clean membrane technology solutions to reduce energy consumption and carbon emissions associated with industrial separation processes. The company's molecular filters, engineered to have exceptional size selectivity, can purify hydrogen and natural gas, generate oxygen-rich air, and perform carbon capture at the point source.
companies.critical_need: The chemical industry relies on century-old, inefficient processes to separate or purify chemicals in order to derive saleable products or remove impurities. Distillation and absorption, which are the most widely used separation technologies, are expensive and energy-intensive, accounting for more than 16 percent of the global carbon emissions. Chemical companies understand that separations generate greenhouse gasses and particulate emissions that contribute to climate change and are harmful to public health. They want new technologies that meet or exceed the efficacy and efficiency of separations based on distillation, which separates mixtures of chemicals by simply boiling liquids or solids into vapors, or absorption, which requires the use of heat and pressure to isolate target elements of mixtures using adsorbents.
companies.technology_vision: Compared to incumbent separation processes, which require large smokestacks and huge energy input, membranes have a small footprint and can be up to 10X more energy-efficient. Membrane pores sort molecules based on their size and chemical affinity as they move through the membrane. Osmoses has developed membrane materials for chemical separations that have exceptional permeability and unprecedented stability. Its vision is to deploy in the energy, chemical, and petrochemical sectors, where its membrane’s high permeability and selectivity can meet customer’s needs for energy savings and gas-product recovery. Osmoses is targeting: -Carbon dioxide removal from natural gas and biogas. Osmoses could reduce energy consumption by more than 40 percent and cut methane loss by more than 70 percent, compared to state-of-the-art commercial membrane systems. -Oxygen-nitrogen separation (in nitrogen and oxygen production) where the technology could cut nitrogen production costs by around 10 percent, relative to leading membrane systems. -Hydrogen purification, where Osmoses’ highly selective membrane could nearly halve the energy costs of hydrogen separations compared to other membranes -Carbon dioxide capture, where the technology could greatly reduce costs
companies.potential_impact: Membrane separation technology that matches the performance of incumbent methods could drastically reduce energy consumption in the chemical, energy, and petrochemical sectors. Using membrane technology to purify hydrogen and renewable natural gas would also accelerate the replacement of coal for electricity generation. Across industries, replacing absorption and distillation with membrane technology for separations can reduce annual U.S. energy costs by $4 billion and eliminate 100 million tons of carbon dioxide emissions. Rebuilding the post-pandemic society requires mass mobilization of resources and presents an opportunity for us to reimagine and rebuild our energy infrastructure while creating new jobs. Green and readily available alternative energy sources, such as hydrogen and renewable natural gas, will only see widespread adoption if cost-competitive with conventional sources. Osmoses wants to be part of this revolution.

nsf_awards.title: SBIR Phase I: Optimization and scaling of ladder polymers for membrane-based gas separations

fellow_profiles.name: Francesco Benedetti
fellow_profiles.bio: Francesco M. Benedetti is co-founder of Osmoses and a postdoctoral associate in chemical engineering at MIT. He earned his Ph.D. in chemical engineering from the oldest university in the world: University of Bologna, Italy. Benedetti received a 2019/2020 MIT Energy Fellowship and was an NSF I-Corps Entrepreneurial Lead in 2021. Benedetti and his team are committed to bringing membrane technologies from the lab to the global market, creating a sustainable future for energy.

fellow_profiles.name: Holden Lai
fellow_profiles.bio: Holden Lai is co-founder and CTO of Osmoses. He earned a B.Mus. in percussion performance and a B.A. in chemistry and materials physics from Oberlin College. Lai earned his Ph.D. in chemistry at Stanford University as an NSF Graduate Research Fellow in the Yan Xia lab, where he developed membrane materials with unprecedented performance for industrial gas separations. At Osmoses, he leads the materials research and development efforts.

companies.name: Pheronym
companies.one_liner: Pheronym uses pheromones from microscopic roundworms nematodes to control agricultural pests in the soil.
companies.elevator_pitch: Pheronym is an agricultural biotechnology company that uses synthetic biology to develop nontoxic solutions for pest management. Using pheromones to control the behavior and development of microscopic roundworms called nematodes, Pheronym offers a sustainable and effective path for farmers as they transition away from conventional pesticides.
companies.critical_need: Without the $60 billion pesticide market, farmers would see up to 80 percent crop loss globally. But in addition to increasing yield, synthetic pesticides have had negative impacts on public health and water quality, and neonicotinoid insecticides are associated with the decline of honey bees and other important pollinators. Because of this, some pesticides are coming under tighter regulatory control and, in some cases, are being banned. To ensure high yields and global food security, farmers need nontoxic, highly effective approaches to controlling pests. Pheromones, the biochemicals that insects and other animals use to communicate, attract mates, warn others of predators, or find food, can serve as a nontoxic tool for pest control. But until now, pheromone-based systems have only been designed for insects, primarily through attracting insects to traps or disrupting their mating, and have only been applied above ground. Farmers need a broader set of tools to leverage pheromones for pest control.
companies.technology_vision: Some nematodes benefit crop plants by preying on organisms that eat the plants, while others reduce crop health by attacking plant roots. Pheronym’s biocontrol solutions regulate both types. Its patented beneficial nematode conditioner provides a safe and effective way to direct beneficial nematodes to attack target crop-eating insects in a pack, resulting in a 3X insect kill rate compared to untreated nematodes. And its patented seed treatment directs plant-parasitic nematodes away from healthy roots. Insect pheromones have been successfully used to control insects above ground, but Pheronym has demonstrated the ability to apply pheromones for pest control under the soil for the first time. Pheronym is developing a manufacturing method using tools developed for in vitro fermentation and synthetic biology to create a manufacturing platform to produce specific pheromone mixtures to control all nematodes. Currently, no such commercial nematode pheromone production platforms exist, despite strong customer demand for effective pheromone-based pest control.
companies.potential_impact: Pheronym’s first product, still in prototype, has already improved beneficial nematodes’ efficacy up to 78 percent, without negatively impacting bees or other pollinators. In fact, Pheronym’s technology could bolster bee health by conditioning nematodes to increase attacks on beehive pests. Using biomimicry and cutting-edge biological control tools to control pests, Pheronym is creating a path for farmers to grow more food more sustainably while also providing them a means of transitioning away from conventional pesticides that are or may become more tightly regulated.

nsf_awards.title: SBIR Phase II:  A scalable platform to produce nematode pheromones for crop protection
SBIR Phase I:  A scalable platform to produce nematode pheromones for crop protection

fellow_profiles.name: Cameron Schiller
fellow_profiles.bio: Karl Cameron Schiller is the COO of Pheronym. He has a B.A. in economics and an M.Sc. in pharmaceutical economics. Prior to Pheronym, he was a consultant in pharmaceutical product development, cost-effectiveness analysis, modeling, and statistical analysis. His clients included the University of Florida, the University of Alabama, Florida Medicaid, and at Pfizer. Along with Fatma Kaplan, he conducted the first agricultural biocontrol experiment in space at the International Space Station in 2020.

fellow_profiles.name: Fatma Kaplan
fellow_profiles.bio: Fatma Kaplan is the CEO and chief science officer of Pheronym. While earning a Ph.D. in plant molecular and cellular biology, she identified the role of beta-amylase and maltose during cold and heat shock. She discovered the first Caenorhabditis elegans sex pheromone and found that pheromones regulate other behaviors in both parasitic and beneficial nematodes. She conducted the first agricultural biocontrol experiment in space at the International Space Station in 2020.

companies.name: Qunnect
companies.one_liner: Qunnect builds hardware to transform telecommunications infrastructure into scalable quantum networks.
companies.elevator_pitch: Qunnect intends to bring to market the necessary components that research and industry need to accelerate the rollout of quantum technologies for quantum internet infrastructure.

nsf_awards.title: SBIR Phase I:  Machine Learning Enhanced Hardware for Optimization and Self-tuning of High-Finesse Optical Cavities

fellow_profiles.name: Mael Flament
fellow_profiles.bio: Mael Flament is an expert in the design of precision scientific instrumentation. Prior to co-founding Qunnect, he conducted research at Stony Brook University, Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory, Brookhaven National Laboratory, CERN, and NASA. Qunnect’s product line is an extension of his graduate work developing a new generation of compact room-temperature quantum memories and quantum networking components in the Quantum Information Sciences & Technology laboratory at Stony Brook University.

companies.name: Revive Labs
companies.one_liner: Revive Labs builds simple and secure key management and recovery infrastructure to help provide resilient digital asset ownership.
companies.elevator_pitch: To onboard the next billion people into cryptocurrency and web3, we need to make it easier and safer to start. Revive Labs is bringing secure digital asset custody to the masses. To protect against social engineering and insider threats, Revive Labs’s technology empowers people through social recovery.

fellow_profiles.name: Andrew Stern
fellow_profiles.bio: Andrew Stern is the founder and CEO of Revive Labs, a digital asset safety and security company. He completed his Ph.D. at the Florida Institute for Cybersecurity Research at the University of Florida. There, his research on the topic of hardware security spanned the semiconductor supply chain, including frameworks for counterfeit detection and avoidance, sub-micron Trojan detection techniques, and semiconductor intellectual property protection in a near zero-trust environment.

companies.name: Semiotic Labs
companies.one_liner: Semiotic Labs is enabling a scalable decentralized Web3 by building secure and verifiable autonomous applications.
companies.elevator_pitch: Semiotic Labs is building a secure protocol to automate negotiations for decentralized markets. Its technology, based on hardware-accelerated cryptography and AI, provides privacy protections as well as intelligent decision-making. It opens new applications for blockchain technology, where eliminating central authorities and intermediaries increases operational efficiency and reduces costs, particularly in high-security applications.

fellow_profiles.name: Ahmet Ozcan
fellow_profiles.bio: Ahmet Ozcan, Semiotic’s co-founder and CEO, is an accomplished scientist and IBM Master Inventor with over 100 patents. With a Ph.D. in physics from Boston University, he managed the Machine Intelligence group and led brain-inspired AI algorithm research at IBM. Ozcan played a key role in IBM Neural Computer’s first application development, and his research spans computer science, cognitive psychology, neuroscience, physics, and microelectronics, with over 50 peer-reviewed articles published.

fellow_profiles.name: Sam Green
fellow_profiles.bio: Sam Green, former co-founder of Semiotic, has a strong foundation in applied math and embedded electronics. Leveraging these skills, he began his career in cryptographic hardware analysis, gaining expertise in statistics and machine learning. This experience paved the way for Green to pursue a Ph.D. at UC Santa Barbara, where he focused on developing AI agents for efficient reinforcement learning and decision-making under uncertainty.

companies.name: Thalo Labs
companies.one_liner: Thalo Labs makes it ridiculously easy to measure and mitigate emissions.
companies.elevator_pitch: Thalo Labs develops cost-effective, real-time, actionable pollution data and emissions capture solutions that revolutionize how companies, buildings, and municipalities measure and reduce environmental pollutants at the source. Using smart software to sense and capture carbon, customers can measure, report, and mitigate their emissions, saving money, time, and carbon.
companies.critical_need: Current sensing and machine perception technologies are largely built around sensors and compute techniques designed for human interpretation. This approach is cumbersome and computationally expensive, generates low-quality data, and usually requires a huge investment in people, money, and cloud resources. The cost and complexity of integrating high-quality perception shut out entire industry segments that could benefit from the technology and can serve as a barrier to making actionable decisions on greenhouse gas emissions. Thalo Labs aims to rethink how we sense and interact with the world with novel physics-based perception techniques.
companies.technology_vision: Thalo Labs is developing novel sensing and perception solutions that rethink how machines and devices understand the world. The current paradigms of sensing and perception, including those used in autonomy and spectral analysis, come with considerable hardware and computational costs, and performance that can sometimes leave much to be desired. Thalo Labs’ physics-based relook at the sensing task brings together state-of-the-art electro-optical sensing hardware with optical techniques to reduce both power and retraining costs, thereby opening up a wide array of applications and industries ranging from remote and greenhouse gas sensing to autonomous machines.
companies.potential_impact: We are in the very infancy of human-robot interactions.These nascent interactions are just beginning to hint at the positive impact that pervasive and exceptional robotic perception can have on the world. By breaking down the current high potential energy cost and difficulty of sensing, we hope to unlock numerous advances across industries and for applications in climate tech, safety, and logistics.

fellow_profiles.name: Brendan Hermalyn
fellow_profiles.bio: Brendan Hermalyn is the founder and CEO of Thalo Labs. He is a scientist and engineer with a background in aerospace, environmental science, and sensing. Hermalyn has led and grown teams of tens to hundreds with annual operating budgets of up to $100 million. He holds a Ph.D. from Brown University and master’s degrees in engineering, mathematics, and geological sciences. His systems have been deployed on land, air, and space—from Antarctica to the moon. After appointments at the University of Hawaii and NASA Ames, he joined Skybox Imaging (acquired by Google) to run its space imaging systems group. Most recently, he led Waymo's camera program and GM/Cruise's autonomous vehicle hardware program.

companies.name: AeroShield
companies.one_liner: AeroShield Materials is making the next generation of energy-efficient windows possible by manufacturing super-insulating and ultra-clear aerogel sheets.
companies.elevator_pitch: AeroShield is making the next generation of energy-efficient windows possible with super-insulating aerogel sheets. Developed by the founders at MIT, AeroShield's material is designed to drop inside existing double-pane manufacturing to create a window 50 percent more insulating, reducing energy use and improving comfort in a lightweight and affordable form-factor.

nsf_awards.title: SBIR Phase II:  Ultra-Clear and Insulating Aerogel for Energy-Efficient Windows
SBIR Phase I: Defect-free manufacturing of ultra-clear monolithic silica aerogels for insulated glass units

fellow_profiles.name: Elise Strobach
fellow_profiles.bio: Elise Strobach has always been motivated by the economic and environmental impact of removing the real-world adoption barriers of pivotal technologies. AeroShield Materials is based on Strobach's doctoral work in mechanical engineering at MIT, where she earned her Ph.D. in early 2020. She also has a B.S. in mechanical engineering from the Milwaukee School of Engineering.

fellow_profiles.name: Kyle Wilke
fellow_profiles.bio: AeroShield's cofounder and CTO Kyle Wilke completed his Ph.D. in 2019 under the direction of Evelyn Wang in the mechanical engineering department at MIT. His Ph.D. focused on surface science and interfacial phenomena, with a particular emphasis on durability of surfaces in extreme environments. He now leads AeroShield's technological development and the scale-up of manufacturing.

companies.name: Concerto Biosciences
companies.tagline: Discovering microbial ensembles that benefit people and plants
companies.one_liner: Concerto Biosciences discovers microbial ensembles to address urgent global challenges across healthcare and agriculture.
companies.elevator_pitch: Concerto Biosciences rebuilds microbial communities in, on, and around us to heal our bodies and our planet. Its platform measures millions of microbial interactions for the first time, allowing it to map interaction networks and find "ensembles", groups of microbes that shepherd damaged microbial communities to health.

federal_agencies.name: Department of Health and Human Services

fellow_profiles.name: Cheri Ackerman Araromi
fellow_profiles.bio: Cheri Ackerman Araromi is co-founder and CEO of Concerto Biosciences. Previously, she was an NIH Kirschstein Postdoctoral Fellow at the Broad Institute of MIT and Harvard. Ackerman Araromi earned her Ph.D. in chemistry as a Hertz Fellow at UC Berkeley where she also served on the Chemistry Graduate Life Committee. She holds a B.S. in biochemistry and Spanish from Calvin University.

fellow_profiles.name: Jared Kehe
fellow_profiles.bio: Jared Kehe is co-founder and CSO of Concerto Biosciences. Previously, he was an NSF Graduate Research Fellow and Siebel Scholar at MIT, where he completed his Ph.D. in biological engineering. He also served as president of the MIT Microbiome Club, copy co-chief of the MIT Grad Blog Editorial Board and was a Broad Institute Communication Lab Fellow. He earned undergraduate degrees in bioengineering and music composition from UC San Diego.

companies.name: Elmworks
companies.one_liner: Elmworks' technology enables scalable manufacturing of high-performance electromagnetics for next-generation grid, consumer, and mobility applications.
companies.elevator_pitch: Elmworks is building scalable additive manufacturing technology for electromagnetics, enabling next-generation grid, consumer, and mobility applications. Advances in semiconductors are poised to revolutionize these industries, but the manufacturing of magnetics is preventing widespread adoption. By enabling low-cost, high-performance magnetics, Elmworks eliminates these barriers and enables deep electrification.
companies.critical_need: As the single largest user of electricity in the U.S. and a critical component in decarbonized infrastructure, electric motors will play a crucial role in the 21st-century world. In parallel, solid state power transformers are poised to revolutionize the electrical grid. Despite this, the manufacturing of these wirewound electromagnetic devices remains capital-intensive, labor-intensive, and performance-limiting. To drive a decarbonized world, we need advanced manufacturing processes capable of producing power-dense , low-cost electromagnetics.
companies.technology_vision: Technology VisionElmworks is developing additive manufacturing for electric motors, transformers, and other electromagnetic devices through high-speed, precision placement of fine wire. By leveraging modern motion control and efficient upstream manufacturing processes, the geometric control and turnkey production common in PCB manufacturing can be brought to electromagnetics, enabling new architectures and dramatic performance improvements. Because of the achievable density and additive scaling, disparate components can become monolithic, and disparate processes can be combined, reducing development costs, uncertainties, and timelines. Elmworks’ process also embeds sensing and control directly into devices, enabling granular feedback control and performance optimization. Applied to electric motors, this technology enables agile manufacturing of next-generation designs, with dramatic increases in achievable power density and material utilization.
companies.potential_impact: Potential for Impact Elmworks’ technology will transform design and manufacturing of electromagnetics from a dark art to a turnkey process, while also enabling new designs with significant performance increases and cost reductions.. Further, Elmworks’ process will also bring high-performance designs to cost-constrained applications, saving significant energy (and money) due to the vast (and growing) amount of electricity flowing today.

fellow_profiles.name: Sam Calisch
fellow_profiles.bio: Sam Calisch is co-founder of Channing Copper, a startup building home appliances engineered for the decarbonized future. He also leads special projects at the nonprofit Rewiring America, which advocates for widespread electrification. Previously, Calisch founded Elmworks, which is commercializing additive manufacturing of precision electromagnetics. He earned his Ph.D. from MIT’s Center for Bits and Atoms. Before MIT, he worked at Otherlab, building analytical and computational tools for advanced manufacturing and energy projects.

companies.name: GigaCrop
companies.tagline: Crafting better seeds for a better future
companies.one_liner: Perlumi is using synthetic biology to improve harvest yield while reducing greenhouse gas emissions.
companies.elevator_pitch: Perlumi is increasing the rate plants convert CO2 into sugars, which will improve plant yield, drought tolerance, and nitrogen use efficiency. Its core technology replaces the plant enzyme rubisco, which is critical to plant sugar biosynthesis but limits how fast a plant can turn CO2 into biomass, with faster enzymes.

fellow_profiles.name: Chris Eiben
fellow_profiles.bio: Prior to founding GigaCrop (formerly Perlumi), Chris Eiben was a postdoctoral researcher specializing in metabolic engineering and enzyme engineering. Eiben received a Ph.D. in bioengineering jointly awarded by UC Berkeley and UC San Francisco. He has many first-author publications in peer-reviewed journals, including in Nature Biotechnology and ACS Synthetic Biology. Eiben has also received several awards, including the prestigious NSF-GRFP fellowship.

companies.name: Irradiant Technologies
companies.one_liner: Irradiant Technologies is harnessing light to to print optics that will transform how people and machines see the world.
companies.elevator_pitch: Irradiant Technologies is unlocking 3D nanofabrication to gain mastery over light never before possible. It is building light-based solutions to problems spanning sensing, telecommunications, computation, and imaging.

nsf_awards.title: SBIR Phase I:  Large nanostructured changes in refractive index in any 3D geometry

fellow_profiles.name: Dan Oran
fellow_profiles.bio: Daniel Oran explores novel uses of light to create everything from experimental artworks to new methods of fabrication. Before earning his Ph.D. from MIT, Oran received his B.A. in natural science and photography from Hampshire College and worked as a photographer, fine art printmaker, and archivist. At the MIT Media Lab he invented a new form of nanofabrication combining insights from historic photographic chemistry with multiphoton lithography and expansion microscopy.

companies.name: METAseismic
companies.tagline: Design and manufacturing of seismic metamaterials
companies.one_liner: METAseismic is advancing the future of mechanical shielding.
companies.elevator_pitch: METAseismic is a new technology platform that can revolutionize the way we resist impacts and vibration. Its mission is to avoid unsustainable waste from conventional protective materials.

nsf_awards.title: SBIR Phase II:  MetaMaterial for Seismic Energy Absorption
SBIR Phase I:  MetaMaterial for Seismic Energy Absorption

fellow_profiles.name: Noemi Bonessio
fellow_profiles.bio: Noemi Bonessio is the inventor of a novel class of metamaterials with advanced vibration-control properties for the seismic protection of equipment, buildings and infrastructure. She has been an NSF I-corps entrepreneur lead, project scientist in the mechanical and aerospace engineering department at UC Irvine, postdoctoral scholar in the structural engineering department at UC San Diego, and research affiliate at the Caltrans SRMD testing facility. Bonessio holds a Ph.D. in structural engineering from the Sapienza University of Rome.

companies.name: Nitricity
companies.one_liner: Nitricity makes distributed, climate-smart nitrogen fertilizer from air, water and renewable electricity.
companies.elevator_pitch: Nitricity makes nitrogen fertilizer from air, water and renewable electricity. Its fertilizer is produced using a plasma-based process without fossil fuels and in a form that can drastically mitigate agricultural emissions from farming fields.
companies.critical_need: Artificial nitrogen fertilizer is the cornerstone of modern society, responsible for feeding our growing population. But for the past century, nitrogen fertilizer has been produced as ammonia (NH3) in facilities using the Haber-Bosch manufacturing process responsible for 1.6 percent of global carbon dioxide emissions. Plus, this ammonia must then be shipped hundreds to thousands of miles to the farms that use it, growing its carbon footprint and the farmer’s costs. Add to this two other hurdles: production costs tied to volatile natural gas prices, and entrenched supply chains that keep the product unobtainable to developing economies. Farmers the world over need a better way to feed their crops.
companies.technology_vision: Nitricity has developed a system for producing nitrogen fertilizer on-demand using just air, water, and electricity. During the fellowship, Nitricity is developing an array of solar-powered fertilizer systems and establishing its product and growth in local California farming communities. For every pound of nitrogen it sells, Nitricity will mitigate approximately four pounds of carbon dioxide emissions by displacing a pound of conventionally-produced and applied nitrogen. Plus, because the fertilizer will be produced onsite, farmers will be empowered to apply nitrogen more frequently, increasing the nitrogen use efficiency of the field. Tailoring nitrogen application in this way has been shown to decrease nitrate runoff by up to 25 percent, which means less loss of product and less pollution of local water supplies.
companies.potential_impact: By using renewable fuel to electrify onsite production of nitrogen fertilizer Nitricity’s vision is to empower farmers to control their own sustainable nitrogen fertilizer supply while also mitigating carbon dioxide emissions, reducing nitrate runoff, and enabling cost-effective nitrogen fertilization anywhere in the world. Within 15 years, Nitricity plans to scale globally with the target goal of mitigating more than 10 million tons of carbon dioxide per year (or ~2 million passenger vehicles each year). In developing regions that currently have limited access to nitrogen fertilizer, Nitricity’s production model will provide a reliable, low-cost, and localized nitrogen source to meet the needs of farmers as they respond to demands from the growing middle class.

nsf_awards.title: SBIR Phase I:  Enabling On-site Nitrate Fertilizer Production: An Absorption Unit to Efficiently and Selectively Capture Nitrates

fellow_profiles.name: Jay Schwalbe
fellow_profiles.bio: Jay Schwalbe is the VP of technology and co-founder of Nitricity. He earned a B.S. in chemical engineering from Cornell University and his Ph.D. from Stanford. He was a member of the first cohort of the Cargnello Lab at Stanford where he studied many different kinds of nitrogen fixation technologies. After collaborating with Joshua McEnaney for several years, they are now focused on bringing the best nitrogen tech to market.

fellow_profiles.name: Josh McEnaney
fellow_profiles.bio: Josh McEnaney is CTO and president of Nitricity, a company devoted to producing sustainable nitrogen fertilizer. McEnaney received his Ph.D. in chemistry from The Pennsylvania State University where he developed novel nanocatalyst materials for water electrolysis. He continued his studies as a postdoctoral researcher at Stanford, developing several new nitrogen fixation and conversion technologies. McEnaney believes Nitricity provides an outstanding opportunity to mitigate up to gigaton-scale global CO2 emissions.

companies.name: Radical Plastics
companies.tagline: A radical solution to plastic pollution
companies.one_liner: Radical Plastics blends a patented, naturally-occurring catalyst with conventional polymers to make affordable plastics that are completely biodegradable in the natural environment and recyclable.
companies.elevator_pitch: Radical Plastics has developed a patented catalyst that renders conventional plastics biodegradable in the natural environment. By blending this naturally-occurring catalyst with regular plastic resins, it provides an economical, drop-in replacement solution for existing, persistent plastics. Radical Plastics compounds are easy to process on existing equipment, they have the same properties as conventional plastics and are still fully recyclable so they support the circular economy. But if these materials leak into the environment, they will cleanly and completely biodegrade. Let's reduce, reuse, recycle AND REDESIGN plastics so we can enjoy their many benefits without polluting the planet.
companies.critical_need: Plastics production has surged from 15 million tons in 1964 to 380 million tons in 2017, and it is expected to double again over the next 20 years. While plastics have come to serve an increasing number of applications, they represent a massive pollution problem: millions of metric tons enter our oceans each year, killing sea life and endangering food supplies. This environmental cost is driving demand for plastics that are more ecologically friendly but have the same performance as conventional plastics. Some bioplastics could reduce our reliance on fossil fuel feedstocks, but these materials are prohibitively expensive and often fail to meet performance specifications. Plus, while some are truly biodegradable, others will only decompose into microplastics that persist in the environment.
companies.technology_vision: Radical Plastics compounds have the same molecular structure and properties as conventional plastics, can be cost-competitive with and made on the same equipment as conventional plastics, while remaining recyclable. The key difference is that Radical Plastics will completely biodegrade when triggered by the sunlight, heat, water, air and in the presence of natural microorganisms. In one example, the degradation in soil is expected to happen within two years in compliance with the European Union’s for biodegradable plastic mulch (EN17033:2018). The company’s near-term plan is to address an application that currently inflicts significant business and ecological costs: agricultural mulch films. Farmers spend around $4 billion annually to apply, remove, and dispose of mulch film. Radical Plastics offers a mulch film that would biodegrade offering identical performance with reduced total cost.
companies.potential_impact: Consumers are demanding environmentally compatible packaging however industry has struggled to meet exacting performance and price profiles. Radical Plastics believes its innovation offers a path forward with materials that perform like and can be recycled along with conventional plastic, but which also mitigates the serious impacts of plastic pollution.

fellow_profiles.name: Kristin Taylor
fellow_profiles.bio: Kristin Taylor has a Master's degree in Plastics Engineering and over 25 years of plastics industry experience, serving in technical, sales, business development and management roles in several multinational organizations including ExxonMobil Chemical, Metabolix, Sabic and Huntsman Corporation. Taylor was CEO of Radical Plastics, 2018-2022, and is now a Director at Closed Loop Partners, a New York based investment firm focused on accelerating the transition to a Circular Economy.

companies.name: Resonant Link
companies.one_liner: Resonant Link delivers wireless power for mission-critical applications like medical devices, smartphones, and electric vehicles.
companies.elevator_pitch: Resonant Link delivers breakthrough wireless power for mission-critical applications like medical devices, smartphones, and electric vehicles. Its wireless charging technology lays the foundation for powering electric fleets, and in healthcare its tech can eliminate drivelines and battery replacement surgeries for medical implants.

nsf_awards.title: STTR Phase I:  Self-resonant Structures for Long-Range High-Efficiency Wireless Power Transfer

fellow_profiles.name: Grayson Zulauf
fellow_profiles.bio: Grayson Zulauf is co-founder and CEO of Resonant Link, a company building the world's highest-performance wireless chargers. He earned his Ph.D. in electrical engineering at Stanford University with a focus on wide-bandgap semiconductors for next-generation power electronics and received the Stanford Graduate, NSF Graduate Research, and ThinkSwiss Research fellowships. Previously, Zulauf was the first product manager at Motiv Power Systems and received B.A. and B.Eng. degrees from Dartmouth College.

companies.name: ReSource
companies.tagline: Plastic from CO2 and inedible biomass
companies.one_liner: Resource Chemical's vision is to replace plastic made from petroleum with sustainably-sourced materials that offer superior performance at lower cost.
companies.elevator_pitch: ReSource Chemical is on a mission to replace plastic made from petroleum with sustainably sourced materials that offer superior performance at lower cost. Made from CO2 and inedible biomass using a groundbreaking chemical technology, ReSource products have significant carbon dioxide reduction potential compared to their petroleum-based and conventional bio-based counterparts.
companies.critical_need: The plastics industry is a major contributor to global carbon dioxide emissions because commodity plastics are made from petroleum-derived monomers. Under pressure to both reduce its environmental footprint and improve the performance of existing polymers to capture new markets, the industry seeks new, sustainable feedstocks. The bio-based monomer FDCA, for example, is a well-known, high-performance building block that can serve as a green replacement for PTA, a common petroleum-derived monomer used to make the polyester known as PET. With a market of more than $100B, PET is used in bottling, food packaging, films and fibers. FDCA-derived polymers have superior gas barrier, mechanical and thermal properties, compared to those made from PTA, which can increase the shelf life of packaged food and beverages while using less plastic. But bringing such new performance-advantaged polymers with lower carbon intensity to market, and at scale, requires new process technologies.
companies.technology_vision: Despite a decade of significant industry investment, the process complexity and high production cost of FDCA has frustrated large-scale commercialization. ReSource Chemical has developed a unique approach to producing FDCA, a key ingredient in polyesters, polyurethanes, polyamides, as well as non-toxic plasticizers, with significant advantages over conventional processes. Building on fundamental academic work at Stanford and process scale-up efforts funded by ARPA-E, ReSource Chemical has a radically simplified proprietary chemical technology to make FDCA from carbon dioxide and inedible biomass. This approach requires just a third of the process steps (and commensurate reductions in capital and operating costs) of state-of-the-art, enabling low-cost and potentially carbon-negative production for commodity-scale applications.
companies.potential_impact: ReSource Chemical is on a mission to replace plastic made from petroleum with sustainably sourced materials that offer superior performance at lower cost. Bio-based chemicals made from inedible biomass have significant carbon dioxide reduction potential vis-à-vis their petroleum-based counterparts. In addition, the enhanced performance of FDCA-based plastics in food and beverage packaging applications offers significant secondary climate benefits through reduced food waste, refrigeration costs, transportation costs, land and fertilizer use, water usage and materials consumption. Improved packaging performance also benefits food safety and access, particularly for vulnerable populations.

fellow_profiles.name: Aanindeeta Banerjee
fellow_profiles.bio: Aanindeeta hails from India, where she studied chemistry as an undergraduate student. She came to the U.S. for Ph.D. studies at Stanford University and joined the lab of Matt Kanan. In her graduate work, she invented a new process for carbon dioxide utilization with applications in polymer and commodity chemical production. Upon completing her Ph.D. in 2018, she continued developing this technology with support from ARPA-E. In 2019, Banerjee and Professor Kanan founded ReSource

companies.name: SiTration
companies.tagline: Ultra-durable filtration membranes for energy-efficient industrial separations
companies.one_liner: SiTration is developing a solution for the low-cost sustainable extraction of critical materials in the lithium-ion battery recycling process.
companies.elevator_pitch: SiTration's core technology enables the efficient extraction of lithium, cobalt, and nickel in a hydrometallurgical process. At scale, SiTration aims to reduce the operating cost for critical materials extraction by over 50 percent, while cutting reagent and energy consumption by 10X.

nsf_awards.title: SBIR Phase I:  Mobile Produced Water Recycling for Reducing Costs in Natural Gas Production

fellow_profiles.name: Brendan Smith
fellow_profiles.bio: Brendan Smith is CEO and co-founder of SiTration, and co-invented the SiTration membrane, a patented electrified filtration technology enabling ultra-efficient recovery of critical materials such as lithium, cobalt, and nickel from challenging process streams in applications including mining and lithium-ion battery recycling. He earned a Ph.D. in materials science & engineering from MIT and a B.S. in nanotechnology engineering from the University of Waterloo.

companies.name: Sublime Systems
companies.tagline: An electrochemical pathway to cement production
companies.one_liner: Sublime makes low-carbon cement.
companies.elevator_pitch: Sublime Systems has developed a breakthrough process to make low-carbon cement (currently responsible for 8 percent of global CO2). The technology replaces the industry's legacy fossil-fuel-intensive thermal kiln with an electrochemical process that operates at ambient temperatures with renewable electricity.

fellow_profiles.name: Leah Ellis
fellow_profiles.bio: Leah Ellis is the CEO and co-founder of Sublime Systems, a company developing breakthrough technology to make low-carbon cement. Leah and co-founder, Dr. Yet-Ming Chiang, developed the technology while she was an NSERC/Banting Postdoctoral Fellow at the MIT Department of Materials Science and Engineering. Leah has a PhD in chemistry from Dalhousie University, was named among MIT Technology Review's 35 Innovators under 35, and is among the World Economic Forum's Technology Pioneers.

companies.name: Sync Computing
companies.tagline: A new chip architecture for optimization problems
companies.one_liner: Sync uses novel mathematical algorithms to instantly and optimally provision cloud resources for today's largest computing workloads.
companies.elevator_pitch: Sync changes the way we interact with today's cloud computing infrastructure, allowing companies and users to choose resources based ONLY on the things that matter most: cost and time. In the process, it dramatically simplifies and lowers the cost of computing in the cloud.

fellow_profiles.name: Jeff Chou
fellow_profiles.bio: Jeffrey Chou is currently the VP of Machine Learning and AI at Capital One and the former co-founder and CEO of Sync Computing, which was acquired by Capital One in 2025. He received his Ph.D. in electrical engineering and computer science from UC Berkeley as an NDSEG fellow and was a Batelle postdoctoral scholar at MIT, with an emphasis on optical devices and materials for energy conversion. Most recently, Chou was a technical staff member at MIT Lincoln Laboratory with a research focus on novel computing paradigms, nanophotonics, and computational materials science.

fellow_profiles.name: Suraj Bramhavar
fellow_profiles.bio: Suraj Bramhavar is co-founder and CTO of Sync Computing. Previously, he held research positions at MIT Lincoln Laboratory and Intel Labs, where his research focused on integrated photonics for next-generation computing systems. He received his B.S., M.S., and Ph.D. in electrical engineering from Boston University.

companies.name: Umaro Foods
companies.one_liner: UMARO Foods is making seaweed protein the most abundant, accessible, and affordable protein source on earth.
companies.elevator_pitch: UMARO Foods is making seaweed protein the most abundant, accessible, and affordable protein source on earth. Its first product using seaweed protein is the holy grail of plant-based meat: a delicious crispy, crunchy, bacon.

fellow_profiles.name: Amanda Stiles
fellow_profiles.bio: Amanda Stiles is a plant biochemist with a mission to transform our food production towards plant-based protein and away from factory-farmed animals. Prior to UMARO Foods, she led the research team at Ripple Foods, where she developed and patented the process for making Ripptein, a highly purified pea protein. Stiles has a Ph.D. in plant molecular biology from Virginia Tech, where she also earned her B.S. in biology.

companies.name: Zanskar
companies.one_liner: Zanskar is a geothermal discovery platform, leveraging big data and advanced seismic imaging to identify and de-risk geothermal resources.
companies.elevator_pitch: Zanskar’s proprietary suite of subsurface imaging tools combines AI, big data, and advanced sensing to pinpoint the most prolific geothermal systems on the globe. Their technology aims to catalyze geothermal energy as a key component of a 100% carbon-free electric grid.

fellow_profiles.name: Carl Hoiland
fellow_profiles.bio: Zanskar co-founder Carl Hoiland is an exploration geologist with experience researching the effects and timescales of plate tectonic interactions, including how those interactions control natural resource distributions. Previously, he performed hydrocarbon exploration with Shell and collaborated on the structural-stratigraphic framework of copper deposits in northern Alaska with Trilogy Metals. He holds a Ph.D. from the Department of Geological Sciences at Stanford University where he was an NSF Graduate Research Fellow.

fellow_profiles.name: Joel Edwards
fellow_profiles.bio: Joel Edwards is a geophysicist with a background in subsurface imaging and geothermal exploration. Prior to co-founding Zanskar, he worked with Raser Technologies (now Cyrq Energy), BM Geothermal, and was a research assistant at the Great Basin Center for Geothermal Energy, and a graduate student with US Geothermal on the Neal Hot Springs geothermal field. Edwards earned a Ph.D. in earth sciences from UC Santa Cruz and an M.S. from the University of Nevada, Reno.

companies.name: ZestBio
companies.tagline: Turning food waste into biobased products
companies.one_liner: ZestBio manufactures and formulates specialty chemicals for water treatment and cleaning applications from industrial fruit and vegetable wastes.
companies.elevator_pitch: Zestbio manufactures chemicals from the perennial 200 billion pounds of peel/pulp byproduct made by the citrus and sugar beet processing industry. Using synthetic biology to derivatize agricultural byproducts, Zestbio produces chemicals for water treatment, cleaning products, and cosmetics that are currently imported from abroad and derived from petroleum-based processes.

nsf_awards.title: SBIR Phase II:  Microbial conversion of pectin-rich agricultural byproducts into specialty chemicals
SBIR Phase I:  Microbial conversion of pectin-rich agricultural waste into specialty chemicals

fellow_profiles.name: Luke Latimer
fellow_profiles.bio: Luke Latimer is co-founder and CTO of ZestBio. He has led technology, scaling, and product development efforts at Zestbio including NSF and USDA SBIR awards. Prior to founding ZestBio in 2017, Latimer earned his Ph.D. in chemistry from UC Berkeley.

fellow_profiles.name: Ryan Protzko
fellow_profiles.bio: Ryan Protzko is co-founder at ZestBio. Protzko earned his Ph.D. in Molecular and Cell Biology from UC Berkeley. His work has been published in high impact journals and he is an inventor on over 45 patents.

companies.name: Aralez Bio
companies.one_liner: Aralez Bio uses engineered enzymes to make valuable chemical products and to expand the scope of products that can be produced bio-renewably.
companies.elevator_pitch: Aralez Bio invented and commercialized an enzymatic platform to synthesize noncanonical amino acids (ncAAs). These unnatural building blocks are a $1.3 billion market and are found in 12 percent of the top 200 grossing pharmaceuticals, with additional applications in synthetic biology and agrochemicals. Despite their utility, ncAAs are extremely challenging to synthesize: state-of-the-art methods are laborious, multi-step procedures that generate 100 times more waste than product, necessitate the mining and import of rare earth metals, and expose scientists to hazardous working conditions. In contrast, Aralez's engineered enzymes can manufacture hundreds of enantiopure ncAAs in a single step, with water as the only byproduct. This technology merges the benefits of industrial enzymes--efficiency, specificity, and waste reduction--with the versatility of chemical manufacturing to provide sustainable routes to useful products.

fellow_profiles.name: Tina Boville
fellow_profiles.bio: Christina Boville received her bachelor's degree in molecular biology from the University of Colorado. After working at Amgen for two years, Boville returned to the University of Colorado and earned her Ph.D. in biochemistry in Hubert Yin's group. She then joined Nobel laureate Frances Arnold's lab at Caltech as a Resnick Prize Postdoctoral Fellow. Her current research focuses on using directed evolution to train enzymes that make useful products in a sustainable way.

companies.name: Brimstone
companies.tagline: Cleaner ways to make industry proven materials
companies.one_liner: Brimstone is developing carbon-negative ordinary Portland cement that is capable of reducing greenhouse gas emissions by up to 15 percent.
companies.elevator_pitch: Cement production is responsible for 5.5 percent of global GHG emissions, the same as cars. Brimstone is building a technology that can make the exact same cement product that is on the market today but with negative emissions and at cost parity.

nsf_awards.title: SBIR Phase I:  Reducing Emissions and Cost in the Production of Conventional Cementitious Materials

fellow_profiles.name: Cody Finke
fellow_profiles.bio: Cody Finke earned a Ph.D. in environmental science and engineering under Prof. Michael Hoffmann at Caltech.
